title: Universal Analog Quantum Simulation
authors: Yiming Huang, Jiaxing Song, Xiaoxia Cai, Xiao Yuan
abstract: Analog quantum simulators emulate complex many-body dynamics through native continuous-time evolution under hardware-defined interactions. Yet once a platform is specified, its interaction structure is largely fixed by the underlying hardware, restricting the Hamiltonians that can be realized and limiting programmability. Here we introduce universal analog quantum simulation (UAQS), a hybrid framework that systematically expands the range of accessible quantum evolutions within a given analog platform. UAQS employs optimized continuous-time control fields to engineer target dynamics directly, avoiding decomposition into discrete gate sequences. By preserving native analog evolution while extending the set of achievable Hamiltonians, UAQS transforms fixed-interaction analog devices into programmable simulators. Numerical studies on representative architectures, including superconducting circuits and Rydberg-atom arrays, show that UAQS accurately reproduces non-trivial many-body dynamics beyond the intrinsic interaction structure of the hardware. These results establish UAQS as a practical route toward programmable analog quantum simulation.
summary: The paper introduces Universal Analog Quantum Simulation (UAQS), a method to make fixed-interaction analog quantum simulators programmable. By using optimized continuous-time control pulses, it allows hardware to emulate Hamiltonians beyond its native interaction structure. This approach is shown to be effective for simulating complex dynamics and preparing ground states in systems like Rydberg atoms and superconducting circuits.
main_problem: Analog quantum simulators are limited by fixed hardware-defined interaction structures, which restricts their programmability and ability to simulate arbitrary Hamiltonians.
main_result: The UAQS framework successfully transforms fixed-interaction analog devices into programmable simulators capable of accurately reproducing complex many-body dynamics, including real-time and imaginary-time evolution.
method: A hybrid framework using continuous-time quantum control optimization and McLachlan's variational principle to project target dynamics onto a low-dimensional manifold of states.
model_or_system: The framework is demonstrated on superconducting circuits (transmon arrays) and Rydberg-atom arrays, simulating models such as the Transverse-Field Ising Model (TFIM), XY model, Heisenberg model, and Fermi-Hubbard model.
key_observables: Nearest-neighbor correlations, fidelity, spectral functions, Out-of-Time-Order Correlators (OTOCs), and ground-state energy.
important_parameters: Control pulse amplitudes, evolution time (T), number of tunable parameters (d), and control strength (theta_max * T).
main_assumptions: Assumes the Hamiltonian can be decomposed into controllable Pauli strings and that the initial state has non-zero overlap with the target state.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the UAQS construction and state trajectory; Figure 2 compares simulated vs exact TFIM correlations; Figures 6-9 benchmark expressibility, entanglement, and OTOCs; Figure 13 shows H2 ground-state energy convergence.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the limitations of analog hardware, introduces the UAQS mathematical framework and variational principle, provides estimation protocols for control parameters, demonstrates numerical validity on various many-body models, and benchmarks performance against gate-based circuits.
why_interesting: It provides a systematic way to extend the utility of existing analog hardware (like Rydberg arrays) to simulate a much wider range of Hamiltonians without the heavy overhead of full digital gate decomposition.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: An Illustration of universal analog quantum simulation. (a) The construction of a parametrized analog Hamiltonian driven by a control Hamiltonian Hc with programmable pulses u. (b) The trial state traces a trajectory on a manifold M embedded in Hilbert space. The dynamics follow the projection of the exact time evolution −iH|ϕ⟩, presented by a black solid arrow, onto the tangent space TψM at |ψ(θt)⟩as shown by a blue dashed arrow. (c) The left panel shows the workflow for deriving the evolution equation of the control parameters θ based on variational principles, while the right panel presents a general framework for estimating the elements of the the associated matrices M and V .
Figure 3: Numerical simulation of real-time evolution of different many-body quantum Hamiltonians.

Figure 2: Simulation of the real-time dynamics of TFIM with (a) J = 1 and h = 0.5, (b) J = 1 and h = 1. The left panel displays the nearest-neighbor correlation of simulated and target system during the evolution, while the right panel reports the corresponding absolute error with respect to the exact dynamics. The dashed black curves represent the exact time evolution, whereas the colored markers correspond to UAQS implemented with different analog platforms, namely Transmon, Rydberg Ising configuration, and Rydberg XY configuration.

Figure 4: Performance of UAQS for imaginary-time evolution of the TFIM J = 1, h = 1. The solid gray line shows the exact imaginary-time evolution, while blue circular markers denote the UAQS results. The inset reports the fidelity between the simulated state and the exact state during the evolution.

Figure 6: The experimental results of the site-resolved dynamics of OTOC for two different local operators, i.e. Cxx j , Czz j . The solid curves in each subfigure denote the exact results, and the open circles represent the UAQS results. The top row shows the correlator Cxx j (t) while the bottom row shows Czz j (t). Each column corresponds to a lattice site j ranging from j = 0 to j = n.
